one_line_takeaway: First head-to-head evaluation of open-source prompt-leak detectors (LLM Guard, Vigil, Rebuff): transformer and VectorDB scanners work, but default canary-word checks detect nothing and Rebuff's secondary-LLM scanner is trivially evaded via template injection -- so effective defense needs redesigned canary checks plus combined detection policies alongside model hardening.
key_finding: Transformer-based classifiers were the strongest single technique: LLM Guard (Full mode) reached 0.999 recall but at 0.127 FPR, while Vigil's transformer at threshold 0.98 achieved 0.986 recall at just 0.050 FPR. Signature/heuristic scanners were weak and narrow (Vigil Yara recall 0.248; Rebuff heuristics recall 0.33, and slow). VectorDB scanners gave very low FPR (<=0.01) and even caught leet-obfuscated samples, best when the store was extended (Rebuff extended: 0.93 recall, 0.006 FPR); leet and context-manipulation reliably drop VectorDB recall for classes not in the store. The default canary-word checks in BOTH Vigil and Rebuff detected 0% of prompt leaks (the model does not echo a canary it doesn't treat as part of its 'instructions', and delimiter chars are read as comments); Rebuff's canary, when modified with explicit canary-handling instructions, jumped to TPR up to 0.94 on some classes. Rebuff's secondary-model scanner is evadable via prompt-template injection (forging a 'User string:' field so the LLM scores a benign decoy as a few-shot example), which succeeds even with GPT-4o because the root cause is the injectable template, not classifier quality. As whole solutions, Vigil gave the best Fbeta (zero false positives, recall 0.838), Rebuff was the balanced middle (recall 0.981, FPR 0.034), and LLM Guard maximized recall (0.999) at the highest FPR (0.127). Cross-model ASR: ~2% of malicious samples succeeded against GPT-4o vs ~19% against Claude-3-5-sonnet; GPT-4o's main residual weakness was prefix injection, while Claude most strongly resisted context-ignoring.
core_contribution: An empirical, comparative performance evaluation of three open-source prompt-injection detection solutions -- LLM Guard (v0.3.15), Vigil (v0.8.7), and Rebuff (v0.1.1) -- specialized to the prompt-leak attack scenario (an attacker manipulating an LLM app into revealing its system instructions). The authors evaluate the 7 distinct detection techniques these tools implement (transformer-based classifier, secondary-LLM-based classifier, VectorDB similarity, Yara rules, heuristics substring match, prompt-response similarity, canary word check) both individually and as whole solutions under each vendor's recommended alerting policy. Their own novel findings (as opposed to the surveyed related work): (1) they identify and demonstrate a prompt-template-injection evasion of Rebuff's secondary-model scanner and propose input sanitization as mitigation; (2) they show the default canary-word checks in both Vigil and Rebuff detect ZERO prompt leaks, diagnose the cause, and propose a redesign (adding canary-handling instructions to the system prompt) that restores detection; (3) they propose a custom cross-solution detection policy that outperforms both vendors' native policies on Fbeta and F1. They claim this is the first comparative study of existing open-source prompt-leak detection solutions.
threat_model: Direct prompt-injection, specifically prompt leak (a.k.a. prompt extraction): an adversary submits crafted natural-language user prompts to an LLM-based application in order to make the model output its confidential system instructions (and possibly tooling descriptions), violating confidentiality. Leaked system prompts let an attacker replicate ('steal') the app's functionality or gather intel for follow-on attacks. The target is a self-built LangChain OpenAI-function-calling document-chat agent (backed by GPT-3.5-turbo) that lists and reads documents. Defender's goal: detection-based filtering of malicious user prompts (as one layer among prevention-based defenses). Attacker techniques studied: naive, context ignoring, context manipulation (repeated chars), prefix injection, leet obfuscation, and combinations up to 4 techniques.
limitations: By the authors' own admission: (1) attack samples are derived from manually written bases (keywords like 'your instructions' persist), so they may lose effectiveness as they enter public malicious-prompt databases; they expect token-level optimized attacks (e.g. PLeak) to become the dominant offensive strategy (though those are catchable by perplexity defenses). (2) They did NOT attack real-world deployed LLM applications with diverse system instructions -- only their single self-built document-chat agent. (3) Only three LLMs were tested for susceptibility (GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4o, Claude-3-5-sonnet). (4) Scope is limited to direct prompt-leak attacks; indirect prompt injection and other injection types are left to future work. Reviewer-noted caveats: results depend heavily on the specific tool versions (all in alpha, and the authors note Vigil and Rebuff appear abandoned/unmaintained), the specific vector-store contents, and the chosen beta=1/11 weighting that structurally favors low-FPR/high-precision configurations; the 'Our comb.' policy's superiority is measured only on their own imbalanced dataset.

datasets_used: Self-constructed prompt-leak dataset: 11,000 malicious samples across 11 classes (1000 verified-successful samples each) plus 1000 benign prompts = 12,000 total

11 attack classes: naive (pl), pl_ignore (context ignoring), pl_repeatchar (context manipulation), pl_pi (prefix injection), pl_leet, and combinations up to 4 techniques (e.g. pl_pi_ignore_leet_repeatchar)
Vector store training data: deadbits/vigil-instruction-bypass-ada-002 and deadbits/vigil-jailbreak-ada-002
Transformer classifier: protectai/deberta-v3-base-prompt-injection-v2 (used in both LLM Guard and Vigil)
quantitative_results: Transformer LLM Guard Full mode: recall 0.999, FPR 0.127, F1 0.994; Sentence mode: recall 1.000, FPR 0.157
Transformer Vigil th=0.98: recall 0.986, FPR 0.050, precision 0.995; th=0.999: recall 0.968, FPR 0.014
Vigil Yara recall 0.248 (vs 0.273 expected); Rebuff heuristics recall 0.33, zero false positives
Rebuff secondary-model GPT-3.5-turbo th=0.802: recall 0.914, FPR 0.499; GPT-4o th=0.752: recall 0.868, FPR 0.021; GPT-4o th=0.9: recall 0.638, FPR 0.000
VectorDB: Vigil recall 0.859 FPR 0.01; Rebuff default recall 0.875 FPR 0.007; Rebuff extended store recall 0.93 FPR 0.006
VectorDB per-class recall shows leet/repeatchar degradation, e.g. Vigil pl 85.9% vs pl_leet 55.9%; pl_pi_ignore_leet 86.1% vs pl_pi_ignore_leet_repeatchar 72.6%
Canary word default: Vigil 0.00 TPR and Rebuff 0.00 TPR on all classes; Rebuff modified (canary-handling instructions) TPR e.g. 0.94 (pl_pi_ignore), 0.84 (pl_ignore_leet_repeatchar), 0.41 (naive pl)

Whole-solution totals (FPR / Recall / F1 / Fbeta): LLM Guard 0.127 / 0.999 / 0.994 / 0.989; Rebuff 0.034 / 0.981 / 0.989 / 0.997; Vigil 0.000 / 0.838 / 0.912 / 0.998; Our combined policy 0.016 / 0.981 / 0.990 / 0.998
Cross-model attack success rate on 11,000 malicious samples: ~2% succeeded against GPT-4o, ~19% against Claude-3-5-sonnet
Threshold selection maximized generalized F-beta with beta = 1/11 given the 11:1 malicious:benign class imbalance
baselines_compared: LLM Guard v0.3.15 (transformer prompt-injection scanner, Full vs Sentence preprocessing modes)
Vigil v0.8.7 (Yara-rules, transformer, VectorDB/ChromaDB, prompt-response similarity, canary word scanners)
Rebuff v0.1.1 (heuristics, VectorDB/Pinecone, secondary-model/OpenAI, canary word scanners)
Secondary-model classifier LLMs compared: GPT-3.5-turbo vs GPT-4o
Target/victim models for ASR: GPT-3.5-turbo (primary target) vs GPT-4o vs Claude-3-5-sonnet
Authors' proposed cross-solution combined detection policy vs each vendor's native alerting policy
